title: Find second largest element
description: Given an array find the second largest element

examples.explanation: Step 1: Input array is [3, 5, 1, 5, 2]. Step 2: Sort in descending order: [5, 5, 3, 2, 1]. Step 3: Start from index 1 (value 5). Compare with arr[0]=5: 5 < 5? No (equal). Step 4: Move to index 2 (value 3). Compare with arr[0]=5: 3 < 5? Yes. Step 5: Return 3 as second largest.

Step 1: Input array is [7, 7, 7, 7]. Step 2: Sort in descending order: [7, 7, 7, 7]. Step 3: Start from index 1 (value 7). Compare with arr[0]=7: 7 < 7? No. Step 4: Move to index 2 (value 7). Compare: 7 < 7? No. Step 5: Move to index 3 (value 7). Compare: 7 < 7? No. Step 6: End of array, no element less than first, return None.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the array in descending order and return the second element, handling duplicates by skipping to the next distinct value. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) depending on sorting implementation

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Second Largest Element via Sorting
ladders.desc: Complete solution: Sort array in descending order and return second distinct element.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages sorting to simplify the problem of finding the second largest element. The intuition is that after sorting the array in descending order, the largest elements will be at the beginning. However, we must handle duplicates carefully—if the first element is repeated, the second largest might be further down the array. The algorithm first sorts the array in non-increasing order. Then, it iterates from the second element onward, looking for the first element that is strictly less than the first element. This ensures we skip duplicates of the largest value. If no such element exists (e.g., all elements are equal), we return an appropriate indicator like None or -1. The time complexity is O(n log n) due to sorting, and space complexity is O(1) if sorting in-place, or O(n) if using a stable sort that requires extra space. This connects to L1 by using sorting as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates properly (returning the second element without checking if it's distinct from the first) and not handling edge cases like arrays with fewer than two elements. Real code pattern: def second_largest(arr): if len(arr) < 2: return None; arr.sort(reverse=True); for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] < arr[0]: return arr[i]; return None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array is [3, 5, 1, 5, 2]. Step 2: Sort in descending order: [5, 5, 3, 2, 1]. Step 3: Start from index 1 (value 5). Compare with arr[0]=5: 5 < 5? No (equal). Step 4: Move to index 2 (value 3). Compare with arr[0]=5: 3 < 5? Yes. Step 5: Return 3 as second largest.

Step 1: Input array is [7, 7, 7, 7]. Step 2: Sort in descending order: [7, 7, 7, 7]. Step 3: Start from index 1 (value 7). Compare with arr[0]=7: 7 < 7? No. Step 4: Move to index 2 (value 7). Compare: 7 < 7? No. Step 5: Move to index 3 (value 7). Compare: 7 < 7? No. Step 6: End of array, no element less than first, return None.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sort Array in Descending Order
ladders.desc: Sort the array from largest to smallest to position elements for easy access.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute-force approach: sorting the array in descending order. The intuition is that by rearranging elements in non-increasing order, we can directly access the largest values at the beginning. This simplifies finding the second largest because we only need to look near the start, but we must handle duplicates. The algorithm uses a standard sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort, mergesort, or built-in sort) configured for descending order. For example, in Python, we can use arr.sort(reverse=True). The time complexity is O(n log n) for comparison-based sorts, and space complexity is O(1) for in-place sorts or O(n) for others. This feeds into L0 by providing the sorted array as input for the second-largest search. Common mistakes include using ascending order and then trying to access from the end (which works but is less intuitive) or not considering stable vs. unstable sorts. Real code pattern: def sort_descending(arr): arr.sort(reverse=True); return arr.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array is [4, 2, 8, 6]. Step 2: Apply descending sort. Step 3: Compare elements: 8 > 6 > 4 > 2. Step 4: Sorted array is [8, 6, 4, 2].

Step 1: Input array is [3, 3, 1, 3]. Step 2: Apply descending sort. Step 3: All 3s are equal and greater than 1. Step 4: Sorted array is [3, 3, 3, 1] (order of equal elements may vary but values are correct).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find First Distinct Smaller Element in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Iterate through sorted array to locate the first element less than the first element.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: after sorting, we need to find the second largest distinct value. The intuition is that in a descending-sorted array, the first element is the largest. To find the second largest, we start from the second element and move right until we find an element strictly smaller than the first element. This skips any duplicates of the largest value. The algorithm initializes an index i to 1. While i is less than the array length and arr[i] is equal to arr[0], increment i. If i reaches the end, return None (no second largest). Otherwise, return arr[i]. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case (e.g., all elements equal), but typically O(1) since we stop early. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by operating on the sorted array. Common mistakes include not using a strict less-than comparison (using <= instead of <) or not handling the case where i goes out of bounds. Real code pattern: def find_second_in_sorted(arr): if len(arr) < 2: return None; i = 1; while i < len(arr) and arr[i] == arr[0]: i += 1; return arr[i] if i < len(arr) else None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sorted array is [9, 9, 7, 5]. Step 2: Start at i=1, arr[1]=9. Compare with arr[0]=9: equal? Yes. Step 3: Increment i to 2, arr[2]=7. Compare with arr[0]=9: equal? No (7 < 9). Step 4: Return 7.

Step 1: Sorted array is [4, 4, 4]. Step 2: Start at i=1, arr[1]=4. Compare with arr[0]=4: equal? Yes. Step 3: Increment i to 2, arr[2]=4. Compare: equal? Yes. Step 4: Increment i to 3, which is out of bounds. Step 5: Return None.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare Two Elements for Order
ladders.desc: Determine if one element is less than, equal to, or greater than another.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves comparing two elements to establish their order, which is fundamental for sorting and finding distinct values. The intuition is that to sort or find the second largest, we need to know how elements relate: whether one is smaller, equal, or larger. This is done using comparison operators (<, >, ==). In the context of the brute-force approach, we use comparisons to sort the array and to check if an element is distinct from the first element. The algorithm is straightforward: given two values a and b, we can evaluate a < b, a > b, or a == b. Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to check if arr[i] is less than arr[0]. Common mistakes include mixing up comparison operators or not considering data types (e.g., comparing strings vs. numbers). Real code pattern: def compare(a, b): if a < b: return 'less'; elif a > b: return 'greater'; else: return 'equal'.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input a=5, b=3. Step 2: Check a < b: 5 < 3? No. Step 3: Check a > b: 5 > 3? Yes. Step 4: Return 'greater'.
Step 1: Input a=4, b=4. Step 2: Check a < b: 4 < 4? No. Step 3: Check a > b: 4 > 4? No. Step 4: Check a == b: 4 == 4? Yes. Step 5: Return 'equal'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Array Element by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve the value at a specific position in an array using its index.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing an element in an array given its index. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element can be referenced by its position (index), starting from 0. In the brute-force approach, we need to access the first element (index 0) and subsequent elements (e.g., index 1, 2, etc.) to compare and find the second largest. The algorithm is simple: given an array arr and an integer index i, return arr[i]. Time complexity is O(1) for direct access, and space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 by enabling the retrieval of elements for comparison. Common mistakes include using out-of-bounds indices (e.g., negative or beyond array length) or confusing zero-based indexing with one-based. Real code pattern: def access_element(arr, i): return arr[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: Array is [2, 4, 6]. Step 2: Index i=1. Step 3: Access element at position 1: arr[1] = 4. Step 4: Return 4.
Step 1: Array is [9, 8, 7]. Step 2: Index i=0. Step 3: Access element at position 0: arr[0] = 9. Step 4: Return 9.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand What an Array Is
ladders.desc: Learn that an array is an ordered collection of elements, each accessible by position.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding what an array is. An array is a data structure that holds a fixed number of elements of the same type in a sequential order. The intuition is that it's like a list of items where each item has a position number (index) starting from 0. For example, in an array [3, 5, 7], the first element (index 0) is 3, the second (index 1) is 5, and the third (index 2) is 7. This concept is foundational because the brute-force approach manipulates arrays: we store input in an array, sort it, and access elements by index. Key properties include: elements can be numbers, strings, etc.; size can be zero or more; and indices must be within bounds. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. This feeds into L4 by providing the context for element access. Common mistakes include thinking arrays start at index 1 or confusing arrays with other data structures like sets. Real code pattern: In Python, an array is represented as a list: arr = [1, 2, 3].

examples.output: Element at index 0 is 10, index 1 is 20, index 2 is 30
examples.trace: Step 1: Given array [10, 20, 30]. Step 2: Index 0 refers to the first element: 10. Step 3: Index 1 refers to the second element: 20. Step 4: Index 2 refers to the third element: 30.
Step 1: Given array [5]. Step 2: It has one element. Step 3: Index 0 refers to that element: 5.

approaches.name: Two-Pass Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: First, find the largest element by scanning the array once, then scan again to find the largest element that is less than the maximum. This avoids sorting and uses simple comparisons.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Second Largest Element Using Two-Pass Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Complete solution scanning array twice to find second largest element without sorting.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem efficiently in O(n) time with O(1) space by making two linear passes through the array. The intuition is simple: first identify the absolute maximum value, then find the maximum among elements that are strictly less than this maximum. This avoids the overhead of sorting while ensuring correctness. The algorithm works because the second largest element must be less than the largest element, so by excluding the maximum, we can find the next largest. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize max1 to negative infinity (or first element). 2) First pass: iterate through array, updating max1 whenever a larger element is found. 3) Initialize max2 to negative infinity. 4) Second pass: iterate again, updating max2 whenever an element is greater than current max2 AND less than max1. 5) Return max2. Time complexity is O(2n) = O(n) since we traverse the array twice. Space complexity is O(1) as we only store two variables. Connection to L1: This uses the 'find largest less than given value' subroutine from L1. Common mistakes include: not handling arrays with all identical elements (should return appropriate sentinel), comparing with equality instead of strict inequality, and initializing variables incorrectly. Real code pattern: def second_largest(arr): if len(arr) < 2: return None; max1 = float('-inf'); for num in arr: if num > max1: max1 = num; max2 = float('-inf'); for num in arr: if num > max2 and num < max1: max2 = num; return max2 if max2 != float('-inf') else None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find max1. Initialize max1 = -inf. Scan: 3 > -inf → max1=3; 5 > 3 → max1=5; 1 < 5 → no change; 8 > 5 → max1=8; 2 < 8 → no change. max1=8. Step 2: Find max2. Initialize max2 = -inf. Scan: 3 < 8 and 3 > -inf → max2=3; 5 < 8 and 5 > 3 → max2=5; 1 < 8 but 1 < 5 → no change; 8 not < 8 → skip; 2 < 8 but 2 < 5 → no change. max2=5. Return 5.

Step 1: Find max1. max1 = -inf. Scan: 10 > -inf → max1=10; 10 = 10 → no change; 9 < 10 → no change; 8 < 10 → no change; 7 < 10 → no change. max1=10. Step 2: Find max2. max2 = -inf. Scan: 10 not < 10 → skip; 10 not < 10 → skip; 9 < 10 and 9 > -inf → max2=9; 8 < 10 but 8 < 9 → no change; 7 < 10 but 7 < 9 → no change. max2=9. Return 9.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Find Largest Element Less Than Given Value
ladders.desc: Scan array to find maximum element strictly less than a provided threshold.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the second pass in the full solution. The intuition: given a threshold value (the largest element from first pass), we need to find the maximum among elements that don't reach this threshold. This directly identifies the second largest element when threshold is the array maximum. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize result to negative infinity. 2) Iterate through each element in array. 3) For each element, check if it's less than threshold AND greater than current result. 4) If both conditions hold, update result to this element. 5) After iteration, return result (or sentinel if none found). Time complexity is O(n) for single pass, space O(1). Connection to L0: This is exactly the second pass in the full algorithm. Common mistakes: using <= instead of < for comparison (would incorrectly include threshold), not handling empty results properly. Real code pattern: def largest_less_than(arr, threshold): result = float('-inf'); for num in arr: if num < threshold and num > result: result = num; return result.

examples.trace: Initialize result = -inf. Scan: 3 < 9 and 3 > -inf → result=3; 7 < 9 and 7 > 3 → result=7; 2 < 9 but 2 < 7 → no change; 9 not < 9 → skip. Final result=7.
Initialize result = -inf. Scan: 5 not < 5 → skip; 5 not < 5 → skip; 5 not < 5 → skip. No element satisfies condition, result remains -inf.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Comparison with Exclusion
ladders.desc: Technique of finding maximum, then finding next maximum by excluding the first.
ladders.explanation: This core logic embodies the two-pass strategy: first establish a reference point (maximum), then find the best among the rest by excluding that reference. The intuition is hierarchical comparison—instead of comparing all pairs simultaneously, we create a ranking: champion (max1) and runner-up (max2). By separating the passes, we simplify logic to basic comparisons. Step-by-step insight: 1) Any element could be max1 or max2. 2) After identifying max1, max2 must be among elements that lost to max1. 3) The largest among losers is the second largest overall. This works because transitivity of > ensures the second largest is indeed the maximum of elements less than max1. Time analysis: two linear scans = O(n). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented by the subroutine that finds largest less than threshold. Common mistakes: trying to do both passes in one loop incorrectly, or not resetting comparison for second pass. Real code pattern: The thinking process: 'Find biggest, then find biggest among those smaller than biggest.'

examples.trace: Step 1: Find max1. Compare: Start with A=10 as candidate. B=15 > 10 → max1=15. C=12 < 15 → no change. max1=15. Step 2: Exclude max1 (15). Remaining: A=10, C=12. Find max among these: compare 10 and 12 → max2=12.

Step 1: Find max1. Start with X=5. Y=5 not > 5 → no change. Z=3 < 5 → no change. max1=5. Step 2: Exclude max1 (5). Remaining: Y=5 (but equal to max1, so exclude), Z=3. Find max among less than 5: only Z=3. But wait—Y is equal, not less, so not considered. Actually, we need strict less than: elements less than 5 are just Z=3. But there's another 5? No, Y equals max1 so excluded. So max2=3? This reveals nuance: we need to handle duplicates properly—the second largest is 5 because there are two 5s. Correction: In exclusion, we should consider elements that are not greater than or equal? Actually, the algorithm uses strict less than, so for [5,5,3], max1=5, then elements less than 5: only 3 → max2=3. But second largest should be 5. This shows the algorithm as described finds distinct second largest. To get 5, we'd need to adjust. For this example, assume distinct values: [5,4,3] then max1=5, exclude 5, remaining [4,3], max2=4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Maximum Element in Array
ladders.desc: Linear scan to identify the largest value in a collection.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental: scanning through elements while maintaining a current maximum. The intuition is iterative champion challenge—start with a candidate (first element or negative infinity), and for each subsequent element, if it's larger, it becomes the new champion. This works because the maximum property is transitive; after checking all elements, the champion must be the overall maximum. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize max_val to first element or a very small number. 2) For each element in array: compare with max_val. 3) If element > max_val, update max_val = element. 4) After loop, max_val holds maximum. Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L2: This is the first pass in the core logic. Common mistakes: initializing with wrong value (e.g., 0 for negative numbers), off-by-one errors in loop bounds. Real code pattern: def find_max(arr): if not arr: return None; max_val = arr[0]; for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] > max_val: max_val = arr[i]; return max_val.

examples.trace: Initialize max_val = 4 (first element). Compare: 2 < 4 → no change; 9 > 4 → max_val=9; 5 < 9 → no change. Final max_val=9.
Initialize max_val = -3. Compare: -7 < -3 → no change; -1 > -3 → max_val=-1. Final max_val=-1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison and Update
ladders.desc: Comparing two values and conditionally updating a variable.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing two numbers and updating a stored value based on the comparison result. The intuition is about maintaining state: we have a current best value, and when we see a new candidate, we check if it's better according to some criterion (e.g., larger), and if so, replace the current best. This is the atomic action in scanning algorithms. Step-by-step: 1) Have variables current_max and new_value. 2) Compare new_value > current_max. 3) If true, set current_max = new_value. 4) Otherwise, leave current_max unchanged. This operation is O(1) in time and space. Connection to L3: This operation is repeated in the loop to find maximum. Common mistakes: using assignment instead of comparison, not using conditional check. Real code pattern: if new_value > current_max: current_max = new_value.

examples.trace: Compare: 8 > 5 is true. So update: current_max = 8.
Compare: 3 > 10 is false. So no update, current_max stays 10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic concept of storing multiple values and accessing them sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: an array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, accessible by index. The intuition is like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0, each holding a value. To process all elements, we start at the first box (index 0), look at its value, then move to the next (index 1), and so on until the last. This sequential access is fundamental to linear scans. Step-by-step for beginners: 1) Array arr = [a0, a1, a2, ...]. 2) Use a loop with index i from 0 to length-1. 3) In each iteration, access arr[i] to get the value. 4) Perform operations like comparison. Time to access one element is O(1), to scan all is O(n). Space for array is O(n). Connection to L4: This provides the elements to compare and update. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., accessing index equal to length), confusing index with value. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): value = arr[i]; # do something with value.

examples.trace: Index 0: arr[0] = 10. Index 1: arr[1] = 20. Index 2: arr[2] = 30. After index 2, stop since length=3.
Start i=0: arr[0]=5 → print 5. i=1: arr[1]=3 → print 3. i=2: arr[2]=7 → print 7. i=3 is not < length, so loop ends.

approaches.name: Single-Pass Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain two variables for the largest and second largest elements while iterating through the array once, updating them based on comparisons. This is efficient and handles duplicates by tracking distinct values.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Second Largest Element with Single-Pass Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find the second largest distinct element in an array using O(n) time and O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: The single-pass linear scan approach efficiently finds the second largest distinct element by maintaining two variables while iterating through the array once. The intuition is that we can track both the largest and second largest elements simultaneously, updating them as we encounter new elements. This works because at any point during iteration, we have the current largest and second largest values seen so far. The algorithm initializes both variables to negative infinity (or a suitable minimum) to handle arrays with negative numbers. As we iterate, for each element: if it's greater than the current largest, we update both variables (old largest becomes new second largest, current element becomes new largest). If it's between the current second largest and largest, we update only the second largest. This ensures we always have the two largest distinct values. The time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the array once, and space complexity is O(1) as we use only two variables. This approach handles duplicates by only considering distinct values for the second largest. Common mistakes include not handling arrays with fewer than 2 distinct elements (should return an error or sentinel value), incorrectly updating variables when elements equal current largest, and improper initialization for negative numbers. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the comparison logic with array traversal to solve the complete problem.

examples.trace: Initialize largest = -inf, second = -inf. Process 3: 3 > -inf → second = -inf, largest = 3. Process 5: 5 > 3 → second = 3, largest = 5. Process 1: 1 < 5 and 1 > 3? No → no change. Process 5: 5 == largest → no change. Process 4: 4 < 5 and 4 > 3 → second = 4. Final: second = 4.
Initialize largest = -inf, second = -inf. Process 10: 10 > -inf → second = -inf, largest = 10. Process 10: 10 == largest → no change. Process 9: 9 < 10 and 9 > -inf → second = 9. Final: second = 9.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Update Largest and Second Largest Variables
ladders.desc: Implement the core comparison logic that updates two tracking variables based on a new element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the single-pass approach, responsible for correctly updating the largest and second largest variables when processing a new element. The intuition is that we need to maintain the invariant that 'largest' holds the maximum value seen so far and 'second' holds the second maximum distinct value. For each new element x: if x > largest, then the previous largest becomes the new second largest, and x becomes the new largest. If x is between second and largest (i.e., second < x < largest), then we update second to x. If x <= second or x == largest, we do nothing. This logic ensures we track distinct values properly. The algorithm in pseudocode: if x > largest: second = largest; largest = x; else if x > second and x != largest: second = x. Time complexity is O(1) per element, and space is O(1). Common mistakes include updating second when x equals largest (which would incorrectly handle duplicates), not handling the case when x is exactly between second and largest, and forgetting to update both variables in the correct order when x > largest. This sub-routine connects to L0 by being called repeatedly in the array traversal loop, and to L2 as it implements the comparison technique.

examples.trace: x=6 > largest=5 → second becomes 5, largest becomes 6.
x=6 > second=4 and x=6 < largest=8 → second becomes 6, largest unchanged.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Two Maximum Values in a Sequence
ladders.desc: Learn the technique of maintaining the top two distinct values while processing elements one by one.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the insight that we can find the second largest element by keeping track of both the largest and second largest values as we examine elements sequentially. The intuition is that instead of sorting or making multiple passes, we can update our understanding of the top two values with each new element. This is efficient because it reduces the problem to a single traversal. The algorithm concept: initialize two variables to represent the largest and second largest seen so far (starting with very small values). For each element, compare it with these variables and update them appropriately: if the element is bigger than the largest, it becomes the new largest and the old largest becomes the new second largest. If it's between the second largest and largest, it becomes the new second largest. This ensures we always have the correct two largest distinct values. Time complexity for processing n elements is O(n), and space is O(1). Common mistakes include not distinguishing between largest and second largest when values are equal, and incorrectly initializing variables for negative numbers. This logic connects to L1 as it defines the update rules, and to L3 as it relies on comparison operations.

examples.trace: Start: largest=-inf, second=-inf. After 3: largest=3, second=-inf. After 7: largest=7, second=3. After 2: no change. After 7: no change (equal to largest). After 5: second becomes 5 (since 5 > 3).
Start: largest=-inf, second=-inf. After 1: largest=1, second=-inf. After 1: no change. After 2: largest=2, second=1. After 3: largest=3, second=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing and Updating Variables Based on Conditions
ladders.desc: Practice updating variables conditionally by comparing a new value with existing ones.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental skill of updating variables based on comparisons, which is essential for the single-pass approach. The intuition is that in many algorithms, we need to maintain state (like maximum values) and update it when new data arrives. Here, we practice with two variables: given current values of 'a' and 'b' (representing largest and second largest), and a new value 'x', we update them according to rules. The algorithm: if x > a, then set b = a and a = x. Else if x > b and x != a, then set b = x. This uses conditional logic (if-else) and comparisons (>, !=). Time and space are O(1). Common mistakes include using >= instead of > (which mishandles duplicates), not checking x != a in the else-if condition, and updating variables in the wrong order (e.g., setting a = x before b = a loses the old a). This connects to L2 as it implements the update step, and to L4 as it uses comparison operations.

examples.trace: x=4 is not > a=5, but x=4 > b=3 and x=4 != a=5 → update b to 4.
x=2 is not > a=2, and x=2 is not > b=1 (since 2 > 1 but x=2 == a=2, so condition fails) → no change.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Numbers and Conditional Assignment
ladders.desc: Master comparing two numbers and assigning values based on the result.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing numerical values and using conditional statements to perform assignments. The intuition is that many algorithms rely on making decisions based on comparisons, such as finding maximums or sorting. Here, we focus on simple comparisons: given two numbers, determine if one is greater than, less than, or equal to the other, and then assign values accordingly. The algorithm in pseudocode: if x > y: set some variable based on this condition. This is a fundamental building block for more complex logic. Time and space are O(1). Common mistakes include confusing comparison operators (e.g., using = instead of == for equality), not handling edge cases like negative numbers, and missing else cases. This operation connects to L3 as it's used in the update conditions, and to L5 as it relies on understanding variables and values.

examples.input: Compare x=5 and y=3, if x>y set max=x else max=y
examples.trace: 5 > 3 is true, so set max = 5.

examples.input: Compare a=2 and b=2, if a>b set result='greater' else if a==b set result='equal'
examples.trace: 2 > 2 is false, then check 2 == 2 is true, so set result = 'equal'.

testCases.input: x=7, y=4, if x>y set z=x else z=y
x=-1, y=0, if x>y set z=x else z=y
x=10, y=10, if x>y set z=x else z=y
x=3, y=5, if x<y set z=x else z=y
x=6, y=2, if x!=y set z=1 else z=0
x=0, y=0, if x==y set z=1 else z=0
x=8, y=9, if x>=y set z=x else z=y
x=1, y=2, if x<=y set z=x else z=y
x=4, y=4, if x>y set z=100 else z=200
x=5, y=3, if x>y and x<10 set z=x else z=y

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Variables, Values, and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand how to store and manipulate numerical values using variables.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basics of variables and values, which are prerequisites for any algorithm. The intuition is that variables are named containers that hold data (like numbers), and we can perform operations on them, such as assignment and arithmetic. In programming, we use variables to store intermediate results, like the largest and second largest elements. The concept: declare variables (e.g., largest, second), assign initial values (e.g., -infinity), and update them with new values. This involves simple operations like setting largest = 5 or second = largest. Time and space are O(1) for these operations. Common mistakes include not initializing variables before use, confusing variable names, and misunderstanding value assignment (e.g., thinking it creates a reference rather than a copy). This foundation connects to L4 as it provides the basis for storing numbers to compare.

examples.input: Set a=3, then set b=a, then set a=5
examples.trace: After a=3, a is 3. After b=a, b becomes 3. After a=5, a becomes 5, b remains 3.

examples.input: Set x=10, y=20, then swap x and y
examples.trace: Start: x=10, y=20. Use temp=x so temp=10. Then x=y so x=20. Then y=temp so y=10.

testCases.input: Set p=7, q=2, then set r=p+q
Set a=5, b=3, then set c=a-b
Set x=1, y=x, then set x=4
Set first=9, second=first, then set first=0
Set a=10, b=20, then set a=b
Set x=3, y=4, then set z=x*y
